en.extract:
The chestnut wood quail (Odontophorus hyperythrus) is a bird species in the family Odontophoridae, the New World quail. It is found only in Colombia.


Taxonomy and systematics
The chestnut wood quail has at various times been proposed or considered as conspecific with rufous-fronted wood quail (Odonophorus erythrops), dark-backed wood quail (O. melanonotus), and rufous-breasted wood quail (O. speciosus). It is monotypic.


Description
The chestnut wood quail is 25 to 29 cm (9.8 to 11.4 in) long. Males weigh 325 to 380 g (11.5 to 13.4 oz) and females 325 to 330 g (11.5 to 11.6 oz). Both sexes have grayish white feathers around the eye, with the female's more extensive. Adult males have a chestnut head and brown back, rump, and wings. The rump has fine black vermiculation. The throat and undersides are rufous. The adult female's crown is dusky brown and its breast and belly are dark gray. The juvenile is similar to the adult female.


Distribution and habitat
The chestnut wood quail is found mostly in the western and central Andes of Colombia and locally in the eastern Andes. In elevation it ranges from 1,600 to 2,800 m (5,200 to 9,200 ft). It inhabits the interior and edges of subtropical montane forest, both primary and secondary. It is primarily terrestrial but has been seen roosting as high as 10 m (33 ft) above ground.


Behavior


Feeding
The chestnut wood quail forages by scratching in leaf litter for roots, seeds, fallen berries, and probably also insects.


Breeding
The chesnut wood quail appears to have two nesting seasons that correspond to the wettest part of the year, March to May and October to December. Nests are domed globes on the ground made of leaves and twigs with a short tunnel entrance. The clutch size is four or five and the female alone incubates the eggs. Family groups stay together for many months after hatching.


Vocalization

The chestnut wood quail's advertising call is "a rollicking, rapidly repeated duet, 'orrit-kilyit...'". It also has an alarm call, "a low 'peetit, peetit...'".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the chestnut wood quail as being of Least Concern. Until 2020 it had been rated Near Threatened. Though its population size is not known and is thought to be declining, those criteria are not critical enough to warrant a more perilous rating.


References

The '''chestnut wood quail''' ('''''Odontophorus hyperythrus''''') is a [[bird]] species in the family [[Odontophoridae]], the New World quail. It is found only in [[Colombia]].<ref name=IOC11.2>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/ |title=IOC World Bird List (v 11.2) |last1=Gill |first1= F. |last2=Donsker|first2=D.|last3=Rasmussen |first3=P. |date=July 2021 |access-date=July 14, 2021 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The chestnut wood quail has at various times been proposed or considered as [[conspecific]] with [[rufous-fronted wood quail]] (''Odonophorus erythrops''), [[dark-backed wood quail]] (''O. melanonotus''), and [[rufous-breasted wood quail]] (''O. speciosus'').<ref name=CHWQ-BOW>{{cite taxon |BOW |citation=Carroll, J. P., G. M. Kirwan, and C. J. Sharpe (2020). Chestnut Wood-Quail (Odontophorus hyperythrus), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.chwqua1.01 |access-date=September 12, 2021}}</ref> It is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC11.2/>

==Description==

The chestnut wood quail is {{convert|25|to|29|cm|in|abbr=on}} long. Males weigh {{convert|325|to|380|g|oz|abbr=on}} and females {{convert|325|to|330|g|oz|abbr=on}}. Both sexes have grayish white feathers around the eye, with the female's more extensive. Adult males have a chestnut head and brown back, rump, and wings. The rump has fine black [[vermiculation]]. The throat and undersides are rufous. The adult female's crown is dusky brown and its breast and belly are dark gray. The juvenile is similar to the adult female.<ref name=CHWQ-BOW/>

==Distribution and habitat==

The chestnut wood quail is found mostly in the western and central Andes of Colombia and locally in the eastern Andes. In elevation it ranges from {{convert|1600|to|2800|m|ft|abbr=on}}. It inhabits the interior and edges of subtropical [[montane forest]], both primary and secondary. It is primarily terrestrial but has been seen roosting as high as {{convert|10|m|ft|abbr=on}} above ground.<ref name=CHWQ-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Feeding===

The chestnut wood quail forages by scratching in leaf litter for roots, seeds, fallen berries, and probably also insects.<ref name=CHWQ-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The chesnut wood quail appears to have two nesting seasons that correspond to the wettest part of the year, March to May and October to December. Nests are domed globes on the ground made of leaves and twigs with a short tunnel entrance. The clutch size is four or five and the female alone incubates the eggs. Family groups stay together for many months after hatching.<ref name=CHWQ-BOW/>

===Vocalization===

{{birdsong|url=https://www.xeno-canto.org/species/Odontophorus-hyperythrus |species=chestnut wood quail}}
The chestnut wood quail's advertising call is "a rollicking, rapidly repeated duet, 'orrit-kilyit...'". It also has an alarm call, "a low 'peetit, peetit...'".<ref name=CHWQ-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the chestnut wood quail as being of Least Concern. Until 2020 it had been rated Near Threatened. Though its population size is not known and is thought to be declining, those criteria are not critical enough to warrant a more perilous rating.<ref name=IUCN/>
